5891.history: During the morning HNoMS Aeger was attacked by German JU-88 bombers from III/KG.4 from the recently captured Sola airfield. Aeger claim 2 bombers shot down (1 credited) before one bomb hit her aft of the funnel. Aeger was beached at Hundvåg and abandoned. 8 men died, 67 survived. HNoMS Aeger was a total loss.

5894.built_by: William Cramp and Sons (Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, U.S.A.)
5894.history: USS Yarnall was one of the 50 overage destroyers chosen to be turned over to the Royal Navy in return for the right to establish American bases on British territory in the western hemisphere. She proceeded to St. John's, Newfoundland, where she was decommissioned by the United States Navy on 23 October 1940, and, that same day, the Royal Navy commissioned her as HMS Lincoln (G 42). The veteran destroyer departed St. John's on 3 November and arrived in Belfast, Northern Island, on the 9th. Lincoln moved from there to Londonderry where she was assigned to the First Escort Group, Western Approaches Command. For almost a year she met troop transport and cargo convoys in midocean and escorted them into ports in the British Isles. Between September 1941 and February 1942, the destroyer was refitted at Woolwich, England. At the conclusion of that overhaul, she was turned over to an Norwegian crew serving as HNoMS Lincoln (G 42) and was sent back across the ocean to serve with the Western Local Escort Force, operating along the Newfoundland coast between Halifax and St. John's. In July 1942, HMS Lincoln became HMCS Lincoln when she was transferred from the Royal Navy to the Royal Canadian Navy though still manned by Norwegians. Her duty in Canadian waters continued until the end of 1943, at which time she recrossed the Atlantic. She departed Halifax on 19 December and arrived back in Londonderry on Christmas Day. Early in 1944, the venerable warship was placed in reserve in the Tyne River. Her service to the Allied cause, however, had not quite ended. On 26 August 1944, she was transferred to the Soviet Navy to be cannibalized to provide spare parts for eight of her sisters previously given to the Russians.
commanders: Cdr. Alan MacGregor Sheffield, RN (23 Oct 1940 - Feb 1941)
Lt. Ronald John Hanson, RN (Feb 1941 - late 1941)

5895.history: In October 1940 the passenger ship Antonia of the Cunard White Star Ltd, Liverpool was requisitioned by the Admiralty with the intention to be converted to an armed merchant cruiser. She never served in this role. On 24 March 1942 sold to the Admiralty, converted to a repair ship until 1944 and renamed HMS Wayland. Scrapped at Troon, Scotland in 1948. Displacement: 13867 BRT.

5905.history: USS Requin was on her way to the western Pacific under the command of submarine ace Commander Slade Deville Cutter when World War II ended. Like most of her Tench-class sisters, Requin did not spend the Cold War in mothballs. Instead, she returned to the shipyard for conversion to a radar picket submarine under Project Migraine. Requin and her sister radar picket subs provided surface battle groups with early warning of enemy air attacks, helped direct friendly air strikes, and provided mid-course guidance for submarine-launched Regulus missiles. In her new role as a submersible radar and control center, Requin's after compartment was filled with state of the art radars, communications gear, and control equipment. When smaller, lighter, and far more capable airborne radars made the Migraine boats obsolete, Requin returned to the role of attack submarine as a fleet snorkel conversion. The radars were removed from the after compartment but her aft torpedo tubes were not replaced. Instead, the compartment became a large berthing space. In the 1960s, Requin participated in joint ASW exercises with many South American navies and was also deployed to the Mediterranean. Requin was decommissioned on 3 December 1968 and stricken on 20 December 1971. Requin is one of only two surviving Tench class submarines. The other is USS Torsk (SS-423) in Baltimore. She is also the only remaining submarine to have undergone the Migraine conversion. Requin conducts youth group overnight encampments.
commanders: T/Cdr. Slade Deville Cutter, USN (28 Apr 1945 - )

commanders: T/Lt.Cdr. William Nolin Deragon, USN (22 Jan 1944 - 24 Jun 1945)
Lt.Cdr. Alexander Louis Redon, USNR (24 Jun 1945 - 24 Aug 1945)

5958.history: Decommissioned 31 May 1946. Laid up in the Pacific Reserve Fleet. Stricken 1 March 1967. Her hull was sold for to be broken up for scrap, her connning tower is preserved at Fleet Admiral Chester W. Nimitz Memorial Naval Museum, Fredericksburg, Texas.
commanders: T/Cdr. Harold Eustace Ruble, USN (5 Feb 1944 - 24 Jan 1945)
T/Cdr. Donald Greer Irvine, USN (24 Jan 1945 - 9 Jul 1945)

5959.history: Decommissioned in 1947. Laid up in the Pacific Reserve Fleet. Recommissioned 18 May 1963. Decommissioned and transfered to Brasil 7 September 1963 being renamed Bahia (S-12). Sold to be put on display at the Brazilian Museum of Naval Technology, Santos, Brazil. Due to financial difficulties this did not materialise and the submarine was sold for scrap instead.
commanders: T/Cdr. Clyde Benjamin Stevens, Jr., USN (12 Feb 1944 - 30 Mar 1945)
T/Cdr. Richard Stottko Andrews, USN (30 Mar 1945 - 1 Sep 1945)

5960.history: Decommissioned for transfer to the R.N. on 31 July 1943. Transferred to the R.N. and commissioned in R.N. service as HMS Atheling on 1 August 1943. Returned to the U.S.N on 13 December 1946. Stricken on 7 February 1947. Sold into merchantile service being renamed Roma. Scrapped in Italy in November 1967.
commanders: T/Capt. Ward Carr Gilbert, USN (12 Jul 1943 - )

5961.history: Decommissioned 21 August 1946. Stricken 23 April 1947. Sold 25 March 1948 to be broken up for scrap.
commanders: Lt.Cdr. Joseph H. Hantman, USCG (18 Jan 1945 - ??? (1))

5962.history: Decommissioned 27 June 1946. Stricken 19 July 1946. Sold 10 November 1947 to be broken up for scrap.
commanders: Lt.Cdr. James A. Hyslop, USCG (15 Nov 1944 - ??? (1))

5963.history: Decommissioned 14 March 1946 and transferred to the United States Coast Guard the same day. Decommissioned by the Coast Guard on 2 August 1946. Returned to the U.S.N. Sold to The Netherlands and renamed Cumulus.
commanders: Edward M Osborne, USCGR (11 Feb 1945 - 10 Oct 1945 (1))

5964.history: Decommissioned 27 June 1946. Stricken 19 July 1946. Sold 2 December 1947 to be broken up for scrap.
commanders: Lcmdr Clarence H Waring, Jr., USCGR (22 Jan 1945 - 27 Jun 1946 (1))

5965.history: Decommissioned 14 March 1946. Stricken 23 April 1947. Sold 22 April 1946 to be broken up for scrap.
commanders: Lt.Cdr. Henry P. Kniskern, USCG (29 Jan 1945 - ??? (1))

5966.history: Used as a training ship before the war. Seized by the Royal Navy on 3 July 1940 while at Portsmouth. Used as a Anti-Aircraft battery by the British. Disarmed in April 1941. Later transferred to the Free-French Navy and used as a depot ship. Expended as part of the Normandy breakwater on 9 June 1944. The wreck lies in position 49.18.37N, 00.17.13W.

5967.built_by: F.&Ch de la Mediterranee (La Seyne, France)
5967.history: Used as a training ship before the war. Seized by the Royal Navy on 3 July 1940 while at Plymouth. Used as an accomodation ship by the Royal Navy. Returned to France in August 1945. Used as a ponton postwar. Sold 21 December 1955 to be broken up for scrap.
commanders: Cdr. Claude Henry Brooks, RAN (23 Sep 1940 - 21 Oct 1940)

5968.built_by: F.&Ch de la Mediterranee (La Seyne, France)
5968.history: Sunk by gunfire from British warships at Mers-el-Kebir, Algeria on 3 July 1940. The ship blew up with severe losses. Total killed were 36 Officers, 151 Petty Officers and 825 Seaman. (Capitaine De Vaisseau Louis Rene Edmond Le Pivain). The commanding officer survived the loss but was injured. The French signed an armistice with the Germans on 24 June, after which her navy no longer formed part of the Allied forces. The Bretagne was therefore neither Allied or Axis when sunk.

5969.history: Damaged and beached at Mers-el-kébir during the British attack on 3 July 1940. Served in the Vichy Navy. Scuttled at Toulon on 27 November 1942. Raised on 11 July 1943 by the Italians and scrapped.

5970.built_by: At.&Ch de la Loire (St. Nazaire, France)
5970.history: Disarmed at Alexandria, Egypt and interned by the British on 22 June 1940. Rejoined the Allied cause and rearmed on 30 May 1943. Used as a training ship postwar. Decomissioned on 17 February 1953. Scrapped in January 1954.

5971.history: The unfinshed Richelieu (Captain Marzin) left Brest for Dakar under her own power on 18 June 1940 escorted by the French destroyers Fougeaux and Frondeur, the ship arrived at Dakar on 23 June 1940. Commissioned at Dakar on 15 July 1940 when only 95% complete. Damaged in the British attack on Dakar on 8 July 1940. Joined the Allies in late 1942. Refitted in the U.S.A. at the New York Navy Yard from February until October 1943. Rearmed with U.S. pattern Anti-Aircraft guns, 56-40 mm (14x4) and 48-20 mm (48x1). Richelieu served with the British Home Fleet from November 1943-March 1944, then transferred to the British Eastern Fleet arriving in Ceylon on 12 April 1944, in time to participate in the attack on Sabang on April 19th. After the war she served of Indo-China. Decommissioned in 1956. Stricken in 1959 and hulked as an accommodation ship in Brest. Scrapped in 1968.

5972.built_by: At.&Ch de St Nazaire-Penhoet (St. Nazaire, France)
5972.history: The unfinished Jean Bart (77% complete) left St.Nazaire under her own power on 19 June 1940 escorted by the brand new destroyers Le Hardi and Mameluk and arrived at Casablanca on 22 june 1940. Jean Bart had only one 15" turret installed. The ship was badly damaged by US warships during the invasion of North Africa in November 1942.After the war the ship was towed to Brest and completion work began there in 1946. The ship was only completed to a modified design in 1955. Saw action during the Suez assault of 1956. Stricken in January 1961. Hulked as accommodation ship at Toulon and scrapped in 1970.

5973.history: Damaged by gunfire from British warships at Mers-el-Kebir on 3 July 1940. Further damaged by exploding depth charges on board the auxiliary ship Terre Neuve moored alongside on 6 July 1940. Scuttled in drydock at Toulon 27 November 1942 to prevent her capture by the Germans. Salvaged in 1945 and sold to be broken up for scrap in 1959.

5974.built_by: At.&Ch de St Nazaire-Penhoet (St. Nazaire, France)
5974.history: Strasbourg managed to escape to Toulon with five large destroyers during the British attack on Mers-el-Kebir on 3 July 1940. Scuttled at Toulon to prevent her capture by the Germans on 27 November 1942. Salvaged by the Italians in 1943 and sunk by Allied bombers on 18 August 1944. Salvaged in 1945 and used as an experimental hulk. Sold in May 1955 to be broken up for scrap.

5975.built_by: F.&Ch de la Mediterranee (La Seyne, France)
5975.history: Refitted in 1935. Mainly used as aircraft ferry from the U.S.A. to France before the French capitulation. Interned in the West Indies at Martinique in June 1940 and demilitarized after American pressure on 14 May 1942. Transferred to the Free French forces on 30 June 1943. Refitted in the U.S.A. as aircraft transport 1943-1944 at New Orleans. The ship was rearmed with U.S. pettern armament; 4 5"/38 DP guns, 20 40 mm AA (5x4) and 26 20 mm AA. Hulked 1948 as a training ship. Later used as a submarine depot ship until stricken in November 1966. Sold 31 March 1967 and broken up for scrap in Italy.

5976.history: Disarmed at Alexandria, Egypt and interned by the British on 22 June 1940. Rejoined the Allied cause and rearmed on 30 May 1943. Decommissioned to reserve in August 1947. Transferred to Arzew, Algeria as a floating base ship for Amphibious Forces. Remained in Algeria until stricken 2 July 1955. Broken up for scrap in 1955.

5977.history: Disarmed at Alexandria, Egypt and interned by the British on 22 June 1940. Rejoined the Allied cause and rearmed on 30 May 1943. Decommissioned 11 December 1948. Used as a pontoon and accommodation hulk at Brest. Stricken 28 April 1961. Sold in 1962 to be broken up for scrap.

5978.history: Disarmed at Alexandria, Egypt and interned by the British on 22 June 1940. Rejoined the Allied cause and rearmed on 30 May 1943. Decommissioned on 1 October 1947. Used at Toulon as a pontoon. Sold in 1974 to be broken up for scrap.

5979.history: Scuttled at Toulon on 27 November 1942 to prevent her capture by the Germans.

5980.history: Scuttled at Toulon on 27 November 1942 to prevent her capture by the Germans. Salvaged on 16 April 1943 by the Italians but broken up for scrap.

5981.history: Scuttled at Toulon on 27 November 1942 to prevent her capture by the Germans. Raised by the Italians on 3 July 1943. Sunk again during an allied air raid in 1944.

5982.history: Scuttled at Toulon on 27 November 1942 to prevent her capture by the Germans. Raised by the Italians on 18 March 1943 and broken up for scrap.

5983.history: Disarmed at Alexandria, Egypt and interned by the British on 22 June 1940. Rejoined the Allied cause and rearmed on 30 May 1943. Decommissioned 19 March 1952.

5984.history: Disarmed at Saigon, Indo-China in December 1941 and used as a training hulk. Sunk by U.S.N. carrier aircraft of Task Force 38 on 12 January 1945.

5985.history: Primauguet was present at Dakar during the attack on 7/8 July 1940. Sunk in Casablanca harbour on 8 November 1942. The ship was under refit and not fully operational, but returned the USN gunfire. The ship was badly damaged and suffered many casualties with 45 crew dead and more than 200 of the crew wounded). (Capitaine De Vaisseau Leon Joseph Marie Mercier) the commanding officer was killed and Contre Amiral Raymond Emile Gervais De Lafond was wounded. After burning the whole night, the wreck was abandoned and became a total loss.

5986.built_by: At.&Ch de St. Nazaire-Penhoet (St. Nazaire, France)
5986.history: Equipped and served as a training cruiser before the war. Interned in the West Indies at Martinique in June 1940 and demilitarized after American pressure on 14 May 1942. Transferred to the Free French forces on 30 June 1943. After the Second World War Jeanne d'Arc was refitted and reverted to her peacetime training role in July 1946. Decommissioned on 16 July 1964. Stricken by the French Navy in 1965.

5987.history: Pluton (Capitaine De Vaisseau Henri Jean Etienne Dubois) was sunk at Casablanca due to an internal explosion while unloading mines. The Commanding officer was killed in her loss. Pluton was intended to be renamed La Tour d'Auvergne on completion of a refit from which she was to emerge as a seagoing training ship for Sub-Lieutenants, but was lost before the renaming could take place.
commanders: Henri Jean Etienne Dubois, FR ( - 13 Sep 1939 (+))

5988.built_by: At.&Ch de St. Nazaire-Penhoet (St. Nazaire, France)
5988.history: Decommissioned in October 1951. Stricken 27 October 1959. Scrapped in 1961.

5989.history: Scuttled at Toulon on 27 November 1942 to prevent her capture by the Germans. Salved by the Italians on 3 March 1943 and renamed FR 12. Damaged by US bombers during an air raid on 24 November 1943. Bombed and sunk on 18 August 1944 in an air raid by B-25s of the 321st Bombardment Group, USAAF. Raised and scrapped in 1952.

5990.history: Scuttled at Toulon on 27 November 1942 to prevent her capture by the Germans. Raised by the Italians on 18 February 1943 and renamed FR 11. The Italians began to refit the ship. This refit was not finished at time of the Italian surrender, and the vessel fell into German hands once more. Damaged by Allied bombers on 24 November 1943 and set on fire, also the ship took a list and rested against the quayside. When Toulon was captured by the Allies in August 1944 the ship was found in this state and although a refit was considered, this did not materialised and the ship was scrapped instead.

5991.built_by: At.&Ch de la Loire (St. Nazaire, France)
5991.history: Scuttled at Toulon on 27 November 1942 to prevent her capture by the Germans. The ship west set on fire and was burning for twenty days. The remains of the ship were scrapped in 1946/1947.

5992.history: While serving with the Vichy Navy the ship arrived at Dakar from Toulon on 14 September 1940. Joined the Free French Navy after the Allied landings in North Africa. Decommissioned on 1 February 1955. Stricken on 2 January 1958.

5993.built_by: F.&Ch de la Mediterranee (La Seyne, France)
5993.history: While serving with the Vichy Navy the ship arrived at Dakar from Toulon on 14 September 1940. Joined the Free French Navy after the Allied landings in North Africa. Decommissioned on 1 May 1957. Served as an accommodation hulk for the submarine school at Toulon. Stricken 31 December 1969.

5994.built_by: At.&Ch de St. Nazaire-Penhoet (St. Nazaire, France)
5994.history: While serving with the Vichy Navy the ship arrived at Dakar from Toulon on 14 September 1940. Joined the Free French Navy after the Allied landings in North Africa. Sold for scrap in November 1959.

5995.built_by: At.&Ch de St. Nazaire (St. Nazaire, France)
5995.history: Chacal (Capitaine De Fregate Jean Emile Noel Estienne) was sunk off Boulogne by German aircraft. The wreck lies in only 2 meters of water in position 50º47'58"N, 01º35'44"E. The Commanding officer was wounded in the sinking.
commanders: Jean Emile Noel Estienne, FR ( - )

5996.history: Jaguar (Capitaine De Fregate Marcel Jules Adam) was sunk off Dunkerque on 23 May 1940 by torpedo's from German motor torpedo boats S 21 and S 23. 13 members of the crew went down with the ship, another 23 were injured.The wreck lies in 8 meters of water in position 51º03'26"N, 02º22'12"E. The commanding officer survived the sinking.
commanders: Marcel Jules Adam, FR ( - 23 May 1940)

5997.built_by: At.&Ch de la Loire (St. Nazaire, France)
5997.history: Seized by the Royal Navy in Portsmouth on 3 July 1940. Transferred to the Free French on 31 August 1940. Stranded and wrecked North of Benghazi on 27 May 1943. The wreck was destroyed on 19 June 1943.

5998.built_by: At.&Ch de la Loire (St. Nazaire, France)
5998.history: Scuttled at Toulon on 27 November 1942 to prevent her capture by the Germans. Raised by the Germans on 22 January 1944 and broken up for scrap.

5999.history: Scuttled at Toulon on 27 November 1942 to prevent her capture by the Germans. Raised by the Italians in March 1943, towed to Italy and renamed FR 22. FR 22 was scuttled by the Italians in La Spezia on 9 September 1943.

6000.history: Scuttled at Toulon on 27 November 1942 to prevent her capture by the Germans. Raised, repaired and commissioned by the Italians. Returned to the (Free) French on 28 October 1943. Decommissioned on 4 January 1954.

6001.history: Bourrasque (Capitaine De Fregate Robert Gabriel Arthur Fouque) was sunk on 30 May 1940 near Nieuport after being mined and finished off by German artillary fire. The ship was participating in the evacuation off Allied soldiers from Dunkirk. The wreck lies in 25 meters of water in position 51º14'58"N, 02º33'02"E.
commanders: Robert Gabriel Arthur Fouque, FR ( - )

6002.built_by: F.&Ch de la Mediterranee (La Seyne, France)
6002.history: Cyclone (Capitaine De Vaisseau Yves François Charles Anne Marie Urvoy de Portzamparc) was torpedoed and badly damaged by the German motor torpedo boat S-24 on 30 May 1940. Scuttled at Brest on 18th June 1940 to prevent her capture by the Germans.
commanders: Yves Francois Charles Anne Marie Urvoy de Portzamparc, FR ( - )

6003.built_by: F.&Ch de la Mediterranee (La Seyne, France)
6003.history: Captured by the British at Plymouth on 3 July 1940. Returned to mainland France by her Britsh crew on 25 August 1945. Stricken 17 February 1950.
commanders: Emil Rene Lavene, FR ( - )

6004.history: Orage (Capitaine De Fregate Roger Vincent Marie Viennot De Vaublanc) was sunk on 23 May 1940 off Boulogne by German aircraft. The Commanding officer was wounded in the sinking.
commanders: Roger Vincent Marie Viennot De Vaublanc, FR ( - 23 May 1940)

6005.history: This ship was undergoing boiler repairs in a northern French port when Germany invaded France. The British towed it across the channel to the Devonport Dockyard where the repairs were completed Seized on 3 July 1940 by the British while moored in Plymouth. Transferred to the Polish Navy on 17 July 1940. Transferred to the Free French on 30 April 1941. Decomissioned 7 April 1949.
commanders: Patrice Brunet, FR ( - )

6006.built_by: At.&Ch de St. Nazaire (St. Nazaire, France)
6006.history: Operated by the Vichy French and based at Casablanca during the Allied landing in North Africa in November 1942. Rejoined the Allied cause. Decommissioned 17 February 1950.
commanders: Pierre Marie Charles O`Neill, FR ( - )

6007.built_by: At.&Ch de St. Nazaire (St. Nazaire, France)
6007.history: Sirocco (Lt.Cdr. Guillaume Rons Cristophe Marie Joseph Michel de Toulouse-Lautrec) was sunk off Dunkerque on 31 May 1940 by the German motor torpedo boats S-23 and S-26.The wreck lies in 38 meters of water in position 51º18'57"N, 02º14'08"E.
commanders: Guillaume Pons Christophe Marie Joseph Michel de Toulouse-Lautrec, FR ( - 31 May 1940)

6008.history: Operated by the Vichy French and based in Morocco during the Allied landing in North Africa in November 1942. Rejoined the Allied cause. Decommissioned 17 February 1950.
commanders: Andre Amedee Hercule Deplanque, FR ( - )

6009.history: Operated by the Vichy French and based at Oran, Algeria during the Allied landing in North Africa in November 1942. Beached off Oran, Algeria on 8 November 1942 after being damaged by gunfire from the British light cruiser HMS Aurora and the British destroyer HMS Calpe.
commanders: Raymond Germain Justin Pares, FR ( - )

6010.history: Operated by the Vichy French and based at Oran, Algeria during the Allied landing in North Africa in November 1942. Beached off Canastel, Algeria (near Oran) on 8 November 1942 after being damaged by gunfire from the British light cruiser HMS Aurora and the British destroyer HMS Calpe.
commanders: Adrien Laurent Gustave De Feraudy, FR ( - )

6011.history: Scuttled at Toulon on 27 November 1942 to prevent her capture by the Germans. Raised, refitted and commissioned by the Italians being renamed FR 31. Returned to the (Free) French Navy at Bizerte, Tunisia on 28 October 1943. Stricken in February 1950.
commanders: Rene Gustave Baudoin, FR ( - )

6012.history: Scuttled at Oran, Algeria on 9 November 1942 during the Allied landings in North Africa.
commanders: Georges Alain Joseph Abgrall, FR ( - )

6013.history: L'Adroit (Lt.Cdr. H.M.A. Dupin de Saint-Cyr) was bombed and sunk in shallow water off Dunkirk at 1200 PM on 21 May 1940 by German Heinkel-111 aircraft. The ship didn't explode and lay on the beach of Malo-les-Bains. Only one man was injured, the rest of the crew was safe and served on shore batteries until the French capitulation. The wreck lies in 17 meters of water in position 51º03'42"N, 02º23'20"E.
commanders: Henri Marie Andre Dupin de Saint-Cyr, FR (29 Oct 1938 - 21 May 1940)

6014.history: Operated by the Vichy French and based in Morocco Casablanca during the Allied landing in North Africa in November 1942. Rejoined the Allied cause. Stricken 10 November 1952.

6015.history: Disarmed at Alexandria, Egypt and interned by the British on 22 June 1940. Rejoined the Allied cause and rearmed on 30 May 1943. Stricken 31 August 1950.
